title: Linux Server Cryptojacking via Exposed Service
description: Response playbook for a compromised internet-facing Linux server running an unauthorized cryptominer. Triggered by sustained 100% CPU, a cloud-cost/utilization alert, outbound connections to a mining pool, or an EDR detection of a known miner. Typical entry is an exposed or vulnerable service exploited for code execution.
author: Cognis Digital

steps.name: Baseline normal process and network behavior
steps.description: Maintain an inventory of expected services per host and a baseline of normal CPU and egress so anomalous mining stands out quickly.

steps.name: Ensure isolation and snapshot capability
steps.description: Confirm the team can security-group-isolate or snapshot the instance out-of-band to preserve evidence before any rebuild.

steps.name: Confirm unauthorized mining activity
steps.description: Validate that the high CPU is a miner and not a legitimate workload, and identify the offending process and binary.
steps.detection: Process listing for high-CPU unknown binaries, outbound connections to known mining-pool ports/domains, and EDR miner signatures.

steps.name: Find the entry vector and persistence
steps.description: Determine how the actor got code execution and how the miner survives reboot.
steps.detection: Web/service logs for the exploited endpoint, plus cron entries, systemd units, and modified rc/profile scripts that relaunch the miner.

steps.name: Isolate the host and block the pool
steps.response: Network-isolate the instance (restrict security group to admin access only) and block the mining-pool domains/IPs at the egress firewall; keep the host running to preserve memory evidence.

steps.name: Suspend abused accounts and keys
steps.response: Disable any local or cloud credentials the actor used or created on the host and rotate keys the host could reach.

steps.name: Remove miner and persistence
steps.response: Kill the miner, remove its binary, and delete the cron jobs, systemd units, or startup hooks that relaunch it; prefer rebuild over in-place cleanup if root is suspected.

steps.name: Rebuild and patch the entry point
steps.response: Redeploy from a known-good image, apply the patch for the exploited service, and restore the workload behind tightened ingress before returning to production.

steps.name: Reduce the attack surface
steps.description: Remove or firewall the exposed service, add a CPU/egress anomaly detection, and verify patch SLAs for internet-facing hosts.
